chunks.text:
Ofcom’s access to services regulated under the Online Safety Act
Ofcom’s access to services regulated under the Online Safety Act
Online safety
Safety technology
Information for industry
Published:
21 January 2025
Last updated:
29 July 2025
Ofcom’s mission is to make life safer online in the UK, especially for children. Our role is to ensure that user-to-user services, search services and other services regulated under the Online Safety Act 2023 (‘OS Services’) have the appropriate systems and processes in place to protect people from harm.
To support this, Ofcom may access relevant OS Services from time to time.
Depending on the context, our reasons for accessing OS Services are likely to include the following: 
to help us to understand and monitor the measures OS Services have in place, and the way in which they work to ensure users are being appropriately protected from harmful material as required under the Online Safety Act;
to help us understand the nature and type of harm that arises on OS Services;
to contribute to our policy development work, highlighting best practice approaches and examining how OS Services are protecting users from content containing harmful material; and,
to enable us to verify and better understand OS Services’ responses to formal information requests.
In some instances, accessing individual OS Services may be a more proportionate way for us to collect information, where information is available on the service, rather than issuing formal information requests. For example, we may wish to access up-to-date service policies and explore user journeys and user tool functionality.   
Where necessary to access relevant functions or content, Ofcom will set up individual accounts using an Ofcom email address. Ofcom will abide by the Terms of Service, unless otherwise agreed with the OS Service provider except where it is not practicable to do so.  
Important dates for Online Safety compliance
The Online Safety Act makes companies that operate a wide range of online services legally responsible for keeping people safe online.
Statement: Online Safety Information Gathering Guidance
We have issued a statement on guidance to help services, and other stakeholders, to understand when and how we might use these powers.
General Privacy Statement – How we handle your personal data
How Ofcom handles your personal data
Online safety – what is Ofcom’s role, and what does it mean for you?
The Online Safety Bill recently became the Online Safety Act, meaning it is now law. This means Ofcom is now formally the regulator for online safety, with a responsibility to help make online services safer for the people who use them.
